speaker: AS

words: Palouse— pretty good sized mill. But their timber holdings, I guess

speaker: AS
words: on, but they had logdrives on this river, at that time I guess they

speaker: AS

words: of this hill around this way, you know, just about where the high

words: the mill andAfrom the mill and on out. Then they had a lot of low

words: wanted to keep that river channel over into the log pond. And, you

speaker: AS
words: know they had a lot of timber — and those days, you know, it just

speaker: AS
words: seemed like everybody thought that they could travel for so many miles

speaker: AS
words: and there was nothing but timber that there was no end to it,\see, it

speaker: AS
words: would last forever— 30 when they would log, they would take these great

words: big high trees and they'd take two or three logs off'n from the

words: butt end upt nd when they got up to where was a lot of branches

words: they'd leave all that, they'd just go off and leave it. They just

words: took the good stuff. Well, as I say, they had a lot of this low ground

words: and around and up»past the depot and from there up the hill past

speaker: AS

speaker: AS
words: come back like this and it just wouldn't hardly get stopped until

speaker: AS
words: that nigger— thksawyer operated the nigger— with one hand and the

speaker: AS
words: signals with his fingers— like this— as to what he wanted. He would

speaker: AS

speaker: AS

speaker: AS
words: lunch box down here or there. This building was all steel structuresteel

speaker: AS
words: girders and columns and everything, and then, of course it was

speaker: AS
words: sheetiron covered. And, of course, wherever there'd be a steel beam

speaker: AS

speaker: AS
words: attention to it until noon. So everybody got to suspicioning somebody

speaker: AS

speaker: AS

speaker: AS

speaker: AS

speaker: AS

speaker: AS
words: soon find out who had that pie!! Well, it just turned out that poor

speaker: AS
words: old Carney, the guy that was driving the horse, the nicest guy in the

speaker: AS
words: he pertty near died running to the John!! He got such a diarrhea

words: from that croton oil that, gosh, he was— But that's the way the guy

speaker: AS
words: son would write the letter and mail it to them. And that's the way

speaker: AS
words: they lived over in that country. Well, this guy said that, gosh, when

speaker: AS
words: he come over there, why of course, he had on nice clothes. And, 'course

speaker: AS
words: the people in that part of the world even right today, they think that

speaker: AS
words: all Americans are millionaires, you know. When you come over to Amer

speaker: AS
words: ica, all you have to do if you need money is _ to go out here

speaker: AS
words: the bush and pick it right off the bush, you see. That's what they be

speaker: AS
words: lieve. So they all thought he was a millionaire. And he said that

speaker: AS
words: there was just any number of real young, real good-looking girls and

speaker: AS
words: women that wanted him to marry them, bring them over here, but, of

speaker: AS
words: course he had committed himself to this other woman. So, he said that

speaker: AS
words: it was in the summertime when he went back there and they would dry

speaker: AS
words: their fruit, they had lots of apricots and that sort of fruit, you know,

speaker: AS
words: and hhey would dry them. He asked them why they didn't can them; they

speaker: AS
words: looked at him, they didn't even know what he was talking about!! So

speaker: AS
words: he tried to explain to 'em about getting these glass jars and preserv

speaker: AS
words: ing their fruit that way, it would have more natural flavor to it when

speaker: AS
words: they'd eat it.

speaker: AS
words: They had never heard tell of it, they'd never even seen

speaker: AS
words: it, you know. 'Course, I guess they d seen some canned in tin

speaker: AS
words: cans, you know, that was shipped in there. So he said that he was going

speaker: AS
words: to go down to the store and get some jars and show 'em how to can some

speaker: AS
words: of this fruit, store it. This little store there they didn't know what

speaker: AS
words: it was either. They said that they could get him some bottles. He said

speaker: AS
words: no, he didn't want bottles, he wanted fruit jars. They didn't have any.

speaker: AS
words: So he had to go to another larger town, he said it was about, I dunno,

speaker: AS
words: about fifty or sixty miles away to another town that was bigger. And

speaker: AS
words: he did get some fruit jars and brought 'em back and showed 'em how to

speaker: AS
words: put up some of this fruit. Well, anyway, in the meantime he was get

speaker: AS
words: ting ready to marry this woman, and, of course he had to get her a pair

speaker: AS
words: of shoes; they pretty near killed her, she had never worn shoes in her

speaker: AS
words: life. And the, of course, he married her and he brought her over here.

speaker: AS
words: And he built a house over here in Onaway. But that woman could neither

speaker: AS
words: read nor write. Nobody in the whole village could read or write except

speaker: AS
words: that one person. And I think that person was a priest. But that's one

speaker: AS
words: reason why when they come here to work they wanted to stay pretty much

speaker: AS
words: together. It kind of looked like the people in the town didn't want

speaker: AS
words: them living intermingled with the rest of the town, but I don't believe

speaker: AS
words: that was the case, at all. I think it was a case that they lived that

speaker: AS
words: way of their own choice, because they could jabber and talk in their

speaker: AS
words: own language and they were happy that way. I think the Japs.had better

speaker: AS
words: education, but it was in the Japanese education, and, of course, their

speaker: AS
words: mode of writing and so on, their script is so much different from ours

speaker: AS
words: that they couldn't, most of them,Aread or write our language, but still

speaker: AS
words: they'd been educated in their own country.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think the Japanese kept a lot of their old country customs here?

speaker: AS
words: I think they kept 'em all. Of course they didn't have any temples or

speaker: AS
words: anything like that, but still I think each Japanese family has a shrine

speaker: AS
words: right in their own home. And they have idols and images and whatever -fiie.y

speaker: AS
words: worship, so I think they carried on all their own traditions wherever

speaker: AS
words: they go.

speaker: SS
words: I know that the Chinesahad a very hard time of it in the early days in

speaker: SS
words: the mining camps and around there. They mined the tailings and they

speaker: SS
words: were the heavy laborers. And I know that the old miners say, and I've

speaker: SS
words: read and people feel that the Chinese were just downtrodden, almost

speaker: SS
words: like animals.

speaker: AS
words: Well—

speaker: SS
words: I'm wondering if it was that way with the Japanese, too?

speaker: AS
words: Well, they were. I'm not going to deny that. And, of course, you take—

speaker: AS
words: it's a funny thing— United States is made up and is populated with

speaker: AS
words: just a conglomerate of every race and creed in the world, you know—

speaker: AS
words: makes up the population of the United States. But, nevertheless, it

speaker: AS
words: seems like, oh— it always was very common— you'd hear somebody would

speaker: AS
words: make a derogatory remark about, "oh, that dumb Swede. That stupid

speaker: AS
words: Swede. That dang Swede." And all that sort of thing, see. Well, when

speaker: AS
words: ever they'd talk about the Italians it was always "That Dago." For

speaker: AS
words: years and years you never heard tell of anybody saying Italian, it was

speaker: AS
words: always a Dagd or a Wop. They'd refer to 'em as a Wop. And, of course,

speaker: AS
words: there was some people that probably didn't have two cents to their name,

speaker: AS
words: that if an Italian family was gonna move in next door, they'd feel real

speaker: AS
words: ly put out about it because they didn't want that Dago living next door

speaker: AS
words: to 'em. Well, that so-called Dago that moved next door would probably

speaker: AS
words: financially and every other way be much better off than this person was

speaker: AS
words: that washere. But that s the way people are.

speaker: SS
words: Was their mixing between the Greeks and, let's say, the Italians and

speaker: SS
words: Japanese population of Potlatch? Was that really kept to a minimum?

speaker: SS
words: Was it minimal, or was it ?

speaker: AS
words: Well, I tell you, I don't believe there was any friction between the

speaker: AS
words: races at all, because, oh, it seemed like each one respected the other

speaker: AS
words: one, and each one more or less stayed in their own compound. They

speaker: AS
words: didn't intermingle too much, although on the job they'd probably work

speaker: AS
words: right side by side on the job and just get along.fine. But, while it

speaker: AS
words: always seemed to me, at least, if there was any prejudice, it seemed

speaker: AS
words: to me like it was probably more on the other side, because these min

speaker: AS
words: ority groupsjthey didn't want to mingle with the others. That's what

speaker: AS
words: it seemed to me like. The reason for it might have been that they

speaker: AS
words: might have felt that they were out of place, they wasn't wanted, or

speaker: AS
words: something like that. Now you take, if they had a public doing of some

speaker: AS
words: kind, maybe you'd say for instance, they had— in the early days they

speaker: AS
words: used to have these ice cream socials and basket socials and one thing

speaker: AS
words: another— well those people could have come and they would have accepted their money

speaker: AS
words: like anybody else's, but they didn't come because

speaker: AS
words: they just felt that they was out of place. You know, it was their own

speaker: AS
words: choice that they didn't come. I know for a fact,'course we don't be

speaker: AS
words: long to the Catholic Church, but then I know that they've had a Catholic

speaker: AS
words: Church here, you might say, ever since the town started, and you take

speaker: AS
words: all of these Italians and the Austrians that were here from bordering

speaker: AS
words: onto Italy, they were all Catholics— you know that was a Catholic

speaker: AS
words: stronghold for two thousand years, but you'd never see one of 'em go

speaker: AS
words: to the Catholic Church. The reason they didn't go was because these

speaker: AS
words: other snooty women that went to church there, they didn't want to

speaker: AS
words: mingle with 'em, at least, they thought that was the case, so they

speaker: AS
words: didn't go.

speaker: SS
words: Were most of them single men? Were there many women, many wives?

speaker: AS
words: There was some. Yeah, there was a few, but.most of them were single

speaker: AS
words: men. I think I told you the other day about the approximate number

speaker: AS
words: of people in this town that kept boarders. Well, you take about three

speaker: AS
words: fourths of the houses in town would keep anywhere from two to five or

speaker: AS
words: six boarders besides the hotel being full and the big hotel annex and

speaker: AS
words: that big boardinghouse and the big boardinghouse annex, full of rooms,

speaker: AS
words: all single men, besides all these people that was keeping boarders—

speaker: AS
words: you can see a big percentage of them were single men, at least men that

speaker: AS
words: were not living with their families. You know a man might be considered

speaker: AS
words: single but still he might have a wife and family somewhere else. So

speaker: AS
words: you take among the Japs that were here; I think I knew of just three

speaker: AS
words: families, that is, there was three that had wives and children, and

speaker: AS
words: the rest were all single. But among the Italians there was a few more

speaker: AS
words: that were married. And amongst the Greeks that were here there was

speaker: AS
words: only, I believe there was only one Greek that was married, and he married an American

speaker: AS
words: born German girl. But there was never any Greek wo

speaker: AS
words: men here.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think most of these folks were thinking more about making money

speaker: SS
words: and going back to the old country?

speaker: AS
words: Yeah. I think that was their main purpose in being here, was to accum

speaker: AS
words: ulate all they could and then eventually going back. And that's what

speaker: AS
words: most of 'em done, too. Of course, now you take with the Japs, why, of

speaker: AS
words: course they started thinning out well, about the time that the coun

speaker: AS
words: try went into that depression in the early '30's— well, then the Japs cell

speaker: AS
words: dissipated. And when World War II broke out there was just one Jap

speaker: AS
words: here; a single man, and he was a janitor.

speaker: SS
words: In the store?

speaker: AS
words: Yeah,

speaker: SS
words: Yeah, I've heard of him.

speaker: AS
words: He was the only Jap left here at the beginning of the second World War.

speaker: SS
words: Someone has told me that there was some suspicion about him. Did you

speaker: SS
words: hear about that?

speaker: AS
words: Oh, yeah, yeah. But you know they had this big Japanese concentration

speaker: AS
words: camp down here at Tule Lake, California where they had thousands and

speaker: AS
words: thousands of Japanese, like say, Japanese-Americans interned there dur

speaker: AS
words: ing the war. And they never could find one incident where any of 'em

speaker: AS
words: was disloyal to this country, but still they put 'em all in the pen,

speaker: AS
words: you see. Of course, I suppose they thought they had to do that because

speaker: AS
words: you can't tell just by looking at a person what their intentions are.

speaker: AS
words: But this one that was here; we heard a lot of stories about him, but

speaker: AS
words: there was none of 'em that was ever proven correct. Because, it seems

speaker: AS
words: like that's just a favorite American pastime, you know, is to concoct

speaker: AS
words: some yarn about somebody or something, you know!

speaker: SS
words: Did you know any of the Japanese workers?

speaker: AS
words: Oh, yeah. I knew quite a few of 'em personally. In fact, I worked

speaker: AS
words: in the same area and so on, and we'd meet on the street; we'd speak,

speaker: AS
words: you know. That is, we'd say "hello" or something. We didn't really

speaker: AS
words: stop and carry on any conversations, but— oh, yeah. Well, most of

speaker: AS
words: the Japs and all of the Italians and Greeks, far as I was concerned,

speaker: AS
words: I was friendly with all of them.

speaker: SS
words: There is one other thing I was thinking of—Were the Japanese the least

speaker: SS
words: accepted of the nationalities, because they weren't white?

speaker: AS
words: No, I wouldn't say the least accepted. I think they were probably on

speaker: AS
words: a par with the Italians and the Greeks, as far as that goes. But the

speaker: AS
words: Greeks were a little bit more sociable inclined than the Japanese or

speaker: AS
words: the Italians, seemed to me. Of course, that's mostly because of the

speaker: AS
words: fact they were all single men and they all bached, you know, two or

speaker: AS
words: three of 'em'd have a little house and they done their own cooking and

speaker: AS
words: housekeeping and all that sort of thing. And so, consequently, why,

speaker: AS
words: they would go down to the poolhall or go someplace to spend some of

speaker: AS
words: their time. And that way they hobnobbed with other people more. The

speaker: AS
words: Italians — their social life it seems that they more or less get to-

speaker: AS
words: gether amongst themselves and they didn't spread out and fraternize

speaker: AS
words: with the rest of the town quite as much as the Greeks did.

speaker: SS
words: These married women; would they live right in with the men there?

speaker: AS
words: In the group?

speaker: SS
words: Yeah.

speaker: AS
words: No, no, no. Wherever there was a married couple, they had their own

speaker: AS
words: house. The Japanese as well as the Italians, you know. Those as well

speaker: AS
words: as the Italians, you know. Those that were married, they had their

speaker: AS
words: own house.

speaker: SS
words: Did they do, these groups, different labor than the average people

speaker: SS
words: did in the mill? Did they have a special common labor that they did?

speaker: AS
words: No, not necessarily, but it seemed like the Greeks, they all more or

speaker: AS
words: less centered in on the sawmill part of the plant. And they were darn

speaker: AS
words: good sawmill men, all those Greeks. These two men would ride the car_

speaker: AS
words: riage, you know, the headrig, where they sawed the logs up. Well,

speaker: AS
words: there'd be maybe three fourths of the men— you see on the four car

speaker: AS
words: riages there'd be eight men riding the carriage, two on each rig. And

speaker: AS
words: there'd be maybe half of those men were Greeks. They were real catty

speaker: AS
words: on their feet, you know. Seemed like they got along good with this

speaker: AS
words: motion. And then others were edgermen, trimmermen, and they all car

speaker: AS
words: ried on work that had quite a lot of skill to it. But in the sawmill

speaker: AS
words: unit, you never seen the Greeks out there loading boxcars or piling

speaker: AS
words: lumber in the lumberyard or anything else. But, there's where you'd

speaker: AS
words: see Italians. They'd pile green lumber out in the lumberyard and

speaker: AS
words: they'd pile slabwood in the woodyard and all that kind of work, you

speaker: AS
words: see. The Italians here, they done pretty much the same work as j Poles

speaker: AS
words: did back in the Midwest. And, then, of course, the Japs they all seem

speaker: AS
words: ed to concentrate in the box factory, because the average Jap was quite

speaker: AS
words: a bit smaller in stature than the average man, and they were quick and

speaker: AS
words: nimble and wuick with their hands and all that, and they seemed to fit

speaker: AS
words: into thatv So it seemed like it was sort of a natural area for 'em

speaker: AS
words: to fall into, see.

speaker: SS
words: But you couldn't characterize that by saying that they did the common

speaker: SS
words: labor.

speaker: AS
words: Oh, no, no, no. I'd say that there was quite a lot of skill in all of

speaker: AS
words: it. Now you take, for instance, piling green lumber in the lumberyard

speaker: AS
words: of course, most of that was done by Swedes. I'd say eighty percent of

speaker: AS
words: it was done by Swedes. But then there was some Italians there, too.

speaker: AS
words: And you'd say that that was just absolutely common,grunt labor, but

speaker: AS
words: it isn't. There's a lot of skill in piling green lumber, and doing it

speaker: AS
words: right. Now, you take those alleys in the lumberyard, they were— prob

speaker: AS
words: ably two thousand feet long in that.area, and there was two sets of

speaker: AS
words: track going down each alley, and then, of course, there was piles on

speaker: AS
words: each side, you see,from this side of the track they piled on this side

speaker: AS
words: and this track they piled on this side. Well, these piles were built

speaker: AS
words: up on a sloping foundation, that is the foundation, they were just

speaker: AS
words: some bunks, heavy timber,, but the piles had to be sloped so that any

speaker: AS
words: weather would run off'n the pile, you see. And, of course, the boards

speaker: AS
words: had to be laid on there in courses across. The width of the pile was

speaker: AS
words: usually determined bythe length of the lumber they was piling. But,

speaker: AS
words: anyway they would have— regardless of the dimension of the lumber that

speaker: AS
words: they piled, they used one-by-four as strips, stickers across there,

speaker: AS
words: you see. Every course they d have a strip .in front and then possibly

speaker: AS
words: twothrough the center part, spaces equally and then one at the back.

speaker: AS
words: But, the pile was sloping this way, but the face of the pile was— it

speaker: AS
words: wasn't straight up and down, it was just a little bit in from the per

speaker: AS
words: pendicular, see at the top. So that'd be about the shape of the pile.

speaker: AS
words: Well you can imagine, you stand at one end of that alley and you look

speaker: AS
words: down that alley for two thousand feet and every pile is just the same.

speaker: AS
words: You wouldn't find a variation of an inch in the length of that thing!

speaker: AS
words: And, of course, that was all done, you might say, automatically, them

speaker: AS
words: fellers would slap that lumber down— One man down below there that

speaker: AS
words: had a steel bar that he'd put in the pile and.put these boards up on

speaker: AS
words: here and jack that up, you know, boost it up there, and that fellow

speaker: AS
words: would take it and pull it up and pile it and them piles were high, they

speaker: AS
words: were— if the board, say, was sixteen feet long, that pile was probably

speaker: AS
words: at least twenty feet high or higher, because the man at the bottom'd

speaker: AS
words: push it up and the fellow on the top'd just get ahold of it, he'd pull

speaker: AS
words: it up. But then to see the shape of those piles, it takes skill. So

speaker: AS
words: now, what you do— it requires some skill no matter what your job is—

speaker: SS
words: What about these men that were the head men of the groups? The guys

speaker: SS
words: that took care of the money and that sort of thing? Did they also work

speaker: SS
words: at the same time?

speaker: AS
words: Oh, yeah, yeah. You know, there's always some scuttlebutt, you know

speaker: AS
words: that goes around, and I don't know as you could ever prove anything,

speaker: AS
words: because there's nobody ever tried to prove anything. But there was one

speaker: AS
words: family here and it seemed like the wife was the sharp one, the husband

speaker: AS
words: in that family all he ever done was drive a horse hauling slabwood in

speaker: AS
words: from the plant out to the slabyard and lath and that sort of thing. He

speaker: AS
words: drove this one horse. So he had about the least skill of anybody, but

speaker: AS
words: still he was the fellow that took care of all these financial trans

speaker: AS
words: actions, you see. And, oh, it seems like the scuttlebutt was that he

speaker: AS
words: got to be fairly well fixed financially, because a lot of this

speaker: AS
words: money that was supposed to be shipped to the old country never got there.

speaker: AS
words: Fact of the matter is, it never was sent!! (Chuckles) And so— but

speaker: AS
words: as I say, though, that was rumor.

speaker: SS
words: Well, it would seem that these guys would have a lot of power in a way,

speaker: SS
words: they're acting as the intermediary betwen their men and the management.

speaker: AS
words: Well, as far as the job part was concerned, it seems like each individ

speaker: AS
words: ual would take care of himself as far as the job was concerned, but any

speaker: AS
words: handling of finances and correspondence is concerned, why, — I would

speaker: AS
words: say that they were pretty near a hundred percent illiterate. This guy

speaker: AS
words: would have a payday and he wanted to send ten dollars over to the old

speaker: AS
words: country to his family or his people over there, so he'd give this guy

speaker: AS
words: the ten dollars and told him to send it over there. Well, he could

speaker: AS
words: probably show him a receipt, the money order receipt, but he'd look a

speaker: AS
words: at it and he didn't know whether he was looking at a laundry coupon or

speaker: AS
words: what he was looking at because he couldn't read. He couldn't even write

speaker: AS
words: his 0tin name!! He didn't know whether that receipt was his or whose

speaker: AS
words: it was you see. And he didn't know whether by looking at that receipt

speaker: AS
words: that it went to the individual over there that he wanted it to go to

speaker: AS
words: or it went to Sears Roebuck!! Because he could at that and he couldn't

speaker: AS
words: tell what it was.

speaker: SS
words: They depended on him.

speaker: AS
words: It's pretty easy to flim-flam somebody that illiterate, you know.

speaker: SS
words: In this case the rumor was that it was more the guy's wife that was

speaker: SS
words: doing it?

speaker: AS
words: Yeah. She was sharp. The husband he was just more or less of a big

speaker: AS
words: oxey guy that, he'd drive that horse, then he'd come home and have a

speaker: AS
words: little vino, but his wife she was just upon her toes all the time. She

speaker: AS
words: was full of vim and vinegar! And, of course, she was always manipulat-

speaker: AS
words: ing him as to what he should do, you know. Quite a deal.

speaker: SS
words: Somebody told me that these Japanese contractor that they worked through

speaker: SS
words: he really did have a fairly tight hand with this man that they bought

speaker: SS
words: groceries through him.

speaker: AS
words: That's right. But, of course, he wasn't a resident here. Now, I don't

speaker: AS
words: know, I think he lived in Seattle, and I had seen him a time or two,

speaker: AS
words: and he was a great big fellow. He was as big as two of these other Japs

speaker: AS
words: put together. He was a big man. And he'd come here, you know, and I

speaker: AS
words: know, they'd have some kind of a meeting and he'd straighten out, what

speaker: AS
words: this had to be done and what that had to be done. And I suppose in a

speaker: AS
words: week. We used to hear that these fellows had to pay a percent of their

speaker: AS
words: wages, had to go to him every month, and if that was the case with

speaker: AS
words: Japs scattered in the canneries and everything else up and down the

speaker: AS
words: coast and all around, he probably made a lot of money. But, of course,

speaker: AS
words: that was some of the feudal system that was brought over from the old

speaker: AS
words: country. But that wasn't the case.amongst the Italians, but it was with

speaker: AS
words: the Japs. And take as far as their food was concerned, they bought

speaker: AS
words: some of their food locally at the store here. But the major part of

speaker: AS
words: their food was shipped in by rail. And, of course if you've ever been

speaker: AS
words: in a Jap home or in a Jap camp, boy, it'd just about turn your stomach

speaker: AS
words: soon as you step inside the door. 'Course it probably is still the

speaker: AS
words: same way. You've seen a lot of these split bamboo tubs, maybe so big

speaker: AS
words: in diameter and so deep, you know, with the bamboo hoops on

speaker: AS
words: 'em. Well, they ship fish in those and these little fish are about an

speaker: AS
words: inch long. Little tiny things, they're just about like you see these

speaker: AS
words: little trout just after they're hatched you know, you see. those lit

speaker: AS
words: tle trout in the fisheries, little fellows. Well, that's the way these

speaker: AS
words: fish are. And there's hundred of thousands of them in one tub and

speaker: AS
words: they re preserved or pickled or something, but stink!! My it's just about knock you

speaker: AS
words: down. Well, I've been in that Jap boarding

speaker: AS
words: house that they had here. Fact of the matter is, when I worked at the

speaker: AS
words: mercantile company delivery the groceries, why, I used to make deliveries

speaker: AS
words: up there. And in the Jap camp they never any kind of floor cov

speaker: AS
words: ering at all, it was just the boards, but scrubbed clean, never painted

speaker: AS
words: either, just scrubbed clean right down to the white boards. And then

speaker: AS
words: their tables jjthe same way. You never see any covering on their table,

speaker: AS
words: just boards. Andat each place setting along there where each man

speaker: AS
words: would sit, was just a round bowl about five inches in diameter and may

speaker: AS
words: be three inches deep, or something like that. And then a couple of

speaker: AS
words: chopsticks. And that was the entire placesetting, was this bowl and a

speaker: AS
words: couple of chopsticks. Well, of course, they lived on rice and then

speaker: AS
words: these fish. And, of course, they had some kind of a soy sauce or

speaker: AS
words: something, too to go with it, I don't know what it was. Anyway, as

speaker: AS
words: far as their cooking was concerned it was pretty common and pretty

speaker: AS
words: plain. There wasn't much frills in Japanese cooking. But, anyway, you'd

speaker: AS
